sections.title: Simultaneous Actions: -(으)면서
sections.explanation: -(으)면서 connects two actions that happen at the same time by the same subject. It attaches to the verb stem: add -면서 after a vowel, -으면서 after a consonant. 음악을 들으면서 공부해요 (I study while listening to music). 웃으면서 말했어요 (They said it while smiling). 걸으면서 전화해요 (I talk on the phone while walking). The subject of both clauses must be the same person. -(으)면서 can also express contradiction when the two actions seem inconsistent: 알면서 모른 척했어요 (They pretended not to know even though they knew). This contradictory usage implies criticism — the person is doing two incompatible things.
sections.tip: Same subject, same time = -(으)면서. If the subjects are different, use -는 동안 (while) instead: 엄마가 요리하는 동안 아빠가 청소했어요 (While mom cooked, dad cleaned).

examples.en: I exercise while listening to music
Why do you ask when you already know? (contradictory)

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I read a book while drinking coffee'?
exercises.explanation: Since the same person is doing both actions simultaneously, -(으)면서 is the best choice: 마시면서 (while drinking).

options: They were lying and then smiled
exercises.explanation: -(으)면서 with contradictory actions implies criticism: lying while smiling — the two behaviors are seen as inconsistent or hypocritical.

exercises.prompt: Why can't you use -(으)면서 in '엄마가 요리하___서 아빠가 설거지했어요'?
options: The subjects of the two clauses are different
exercises.explanation: -(으)면서 requires the same subject for both actions. Since mom cooks and dad does dishes (different subjects), you should use -는 동안 instead.

sections.title: Interruption & Transition: -다가
sections.explanation: -다가 indicates that one action was interrupted or transitioned into another. The first action stops (or was ongoing) and the second action begins. It attaches directly to the verb stem. 공부하다가 잠들었어요 (I was studying and then fell asleep). 걷다가 넘어졌어요 (I was walking and then tripped). 서울에 살다가 부산으로 이사했어요 (I was living in Seoul and then moved to Busan). The subject must be the same for both clauses. The nuance is that action 1 was in progress when action 2 began — there is no planned switching, just an interruption or natural transition. -다가 can also be shortened to -다 in casual speech: 먹다 배탈이 났어요 = 먹다가 배탈이 났어요.
sections.tip: Think of -다가 as 'was doing X, then Y happened.' The first action is ongoing and gets cut short. It implies an unplanned change, unlike -고 which is neutral sequential listing.

examples.en: I was watching TV and then fell asleep
It was raining and then stopped
They were working in Korea and then returned to the US
They were laughing and then started crying

options: I was running and then fell
exercises.explanation: -다가 shows that the running was interrupted by falling. The running was in progress when the fall happened.

exercises.prompt: What is the difference between '먹고 갔어요' and '먹다가 갔어요'?
options: 먹고 = ate then left (sequential); 먹다가 = was eating then left (interrupted)
먹고 is formal and 먹다가 is casual
exercises.explanation: -고 indicates sequential actions (finished eating, then left). -다가 indicates interruption (was in the middle of eating, then left without finishing).

options: 공부하다가 친구가 전화했어요 (was studying when a friend called)
공부하다가 친구가 공부했어요 (was studying when a friend studied)
엄마가 요리하다가 아빠가 왔어요 (mom was cooking when dad came)
exercises.explanation: In option A, the same person was studying and received a call (the second event interrupted). While C might seem valid, strictly -다가 works best when the same subject transitions between actions. Here A is the clearest correct usage.

sections.title: Purpose: -(으)려고 and -도록
sections.explanation: -(으)려고 expresses purpose or intention — 'in order to.' It attaches to the verb stem: add -려고 after a vowel, -으려고 after a consonant. 한국어를 배우려고 한국에 왔어요 (I came to Korea to learn Korean). 돈을 벌려고 열심히 일해요 (I work hard to earn money). The subject of both clauses must be the same. -도록 also expresses purpose but is more versatile. It can mean 'so that' or 'to the extent that' and allows different subjects. 학생들이 이해하도록 천천히 설명했어요 (I explained slowly so that the students would understand). 늦지 않도록 일찍 출발하세요 (Leave early so that you won't be late). -도록 can also mean 'until/to the point of': 밤새도록 공부했어요 (I studied all through the night).
sections.tip: -(으)려고 = same subject, personal intention. -도록 = can have different subjects, more about ensuring a result. When in doubt, -도록 is the safer and more flexible choice.

examples.en: I started exercising to become healthy
I studied hard to pass the exam

examples.ko: 다른 사람이 듣지 못하도록 작은 소리로 말했어요
examples.en: I spoke in a low voice so others couldn't hear
Make a note so you don't forget

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I came to the library to study'?
exercises.explanation: The speaker has a personal intention (to study). Same subject, personal purpose → -(으)려고. 공부하려고 도서관에 왔어요.

exercises.prompt: Which pattern allows different subjects in the two clauses?
exercises.explanation: -도록 can connect clauses with different subjects: 아이가 자도록 조용히 했어요 (I was quiet so the child would sleep). -(으)려고 and -(으)면서 require the same subject.

options: I waited to the point my eyes could fall out (waited desperately)
I waited so my eyes wouldn't fall out
I waited until I saw something
exercises.explanation: -도록 here means 'to the extent that.' 눈이 빠지도록 is a figurative expression meaning 'so much that one's eyes could fall out' — i.e., waited desperately/eagerly.
